heading: Best Practices
body:
Use the following best practices to properly manage connections
between [AWS Lambda](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/lambda/latest/dg/welcome.html)
and Atlas:

- Define the client to the MongoDB server outside
  the [AWS Lambda handler function](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/lambda/latest/dg/nodejs-prog-model-handler.html).

  Don't define a new client object each time that you invoke your
  function. Doing so causes the driver to create a new database
  connection with each function call. This can be expensive and
  can result in your application exceeding database connection limits.
  For best performance, follow these guidelines:

  - Create the client object once.

  - Store the object so your function can reuse the client
    across function invocations.

  The [Connection Example](lambda-aws-example) reuses existing
  database connections to speed up communication with the database and
  keep connection counts to the database at a reasonable level with
  respect to application traffic.

**C:**

C

- If you have a Lambda function that connects to a sharded cluster
  with many shards, you might experience performance issues. For
  example, with a ten shard cluster, the driver connects to all thirty
  [mongos](manual/reference/program/mongos) instances by default. You can use the `srvMaxHosts`
  option in your connection string to set the maximum number of hosts
  that the driver connects to. To improve driver performance, set
  `srvMaxHosts=3`. For example:

  ```
  mongodb+srv://<db_username>:<db_password>@<clusterName>.mongodb.net/?retryWrites=true&w=majority&srvMaxHosts=3
  ```

  To learn more, see the [Specify Connection Options](languages/cpp/cpp-driver/current/connect/connection-options)
  guide.

**Nodejs:**

Node.js

- If your handler takes a callback as its last argument, set the
  `callbackWaitsForEmptyEventLoop` property on the
  [AWS Lambda Context](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/lambda/latest/dg/nodejs-context.html#nodejs-prog-model-context-properties)
  object to **false**.

  ```js
  context.callbackWaitsForEmptyEventLoop = false;
  ```

  This allows a Lambda function to return its result to the caller
  without requiring that the MongoDB database connection be closed.
  Setting this property is not applicable for [async handlers](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/lambda/latest/dg/nodejs-handler.html).

- If you have a Lambda function that connects to a sharded cluster
  with many shards, you might experience performance issues. For
  example, with a ten shard cluster, the driver connects to all thirty
  [mongos](manual/reference/program/mongos) instances by default. You can use the `srvMaxHosts`
  option in your connection string to set the maximum number of hosts
  that the driver connects to. To improve driver performance, set
  `srvMaxHosts=3`. For example:

  ```
  mongodb+srv://<db_username>:<db_password>@<clusterName>.mongodb.net/?retryWrites=true&w=majority&srvMaxHosts=3
  ```

  To learn more, see [Connection Options](node/fundamentals/connection/connection-options).

**PHP:**

PHP

- If you have a Lambda function that connects to a sharded cluster
  with many shards, you might experience performance issues. For
  example, with a ten shard cluster, the driver connects to all thirty
  [mongos](manual/reference/program/mongos) instances by default. You can use the `srvMaxHosts`
  option in your connection string to set the maximum number of hosts
  that the driver connects to. To improve driver performance, set
  `srvMaxHosts=3`. For example:

  ```
  mongodb+srv://<db_username>:<db_password>@<clusterName>.mongodb.net/?retryWrites=true&w=majority&srvMaxHosts=3
  ```

  To learn more, see [Connection Options](php-library/connect/connection-options).

- To deploy serverless PHP applications to AWS Lambda, use the
  [Bref](https://bref.sh/) framework. To install the prerequisites
  and configure Bref, follow the [Setup](https://bref.sh/docs/setup)
  tutorial in the Bref documentation.

  Next, you can create a project directory, install the MongoDB and Bref
  dependencies, and initialize the serverless configuration by running
  the following commands:

  ```bash
  mkdir bref-mongodb-app && cd bref-mongodb-app
  composer init
  composer require bref/bref bref/extra-php-extensions mongodb/mongodb
  vendor/bin/bref init
  ```

  Finally, add the MongoDB PHP extension to your project. The `bref/extra-php-extensions`
  Composer package provides a Serverless Framework plugin that adds the MongoDB PHP extension
  as a [Lambda layer](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/lambda/latest/dg/chapter-layers.html).
  Update your `serverless.yml` file to include the following highlighted
  lines:

  ```yaml
  plugins:
      - ./vendor/bref/bref
      - ./vendor/bref/extra-php-extensions

  functions:
      api:
          handler: index.php
          runtime: php-85-fpm
          layers:
              - ${bref-extra:mongodb2-php-85}
  ```

**Python:**

Python

- If you have a Lambda function that connects to a sharded cluster
  with many shards, you might experience performance issues. For


  To learn more, see [Tools for Connecting to MongoDB](https://pymongo.readthedocs.io/en/stable/api/pymongo/mongo_client.html).

**Ruby:**

Ruby

- If you have a Lambda function that connects to a sharded cluster
  with many shards, you might experience performance issues. For
  example, with a ten shard cluster, the driver connects to all thirty

**Rust:**

Rust

- If you have a Lambda function that connects to a sharded cluster
  with many shards, you might experience performance issues. For

  option in your connection string to set the maximum number of hosts

**Scala:**

Scala

- If you have a Lambda function that connects to a sharded cluster
  with many shards, you might experience performance issues. For
  example, with a ten shard cluster, the driver connects to all thirty
  [mongos](manual/reference/program/mongos) instances by default. You can use the `srvMaxHosts`
  option in your connection string to set the maximum number of hosts
  that the driver connects to. To improve driver performance, set
  `srvMaxHosts=3`. For example:

  ```
  mongodb+srv://<db_username>:<db_password>@<clusterName>.mongodb.net/?retryWrites=true&w=majority&srvMaxHosts=3
  ```

* Restrict network access to your Atlas cluster.

  Connect to your Atlas cluster over private networking using a
  Network Peering connection
  between your Atlas cluster and your AWS (Amazon Web Services) Lambda function, or,
  alternatively, a private endpoint,
  so that you can allow only private IP addresses from your
  IP access list.

  If you don't use private networking, consider connecting to your
  Atlas cluster via a [NAT gateway](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/userguide/vpc-nat-gateway.html)
  with a mapped Elastic IP address. Otherwise, you must
  allow all IP addresses (0.0.0.0/0) to access your service cluster.

  > **Warning:** Adding `0.0.0.0/0` to your IP access list
  > allows cluster access from anywhere in the public internet.
  > Ensure that you're using strong credentials for all database
  > users when allowing access from anywhere.

> **Note:** If you add the `0.0.0.0/0` CIDR to a project access list,
> Atlas sends an alert email to all users directly
> granted a role to the project, or indirectly granted a role through
> team membership (if the team is granted a role to the
> project).

- Set [maxIdleTimeMS](manual/reference/connection-string)
  to `60000` to automatically close your connections after 1 minute
  of idle time. Tuning your `maxIdleTimeMS` can help reduce the
  occurrence of timeout errors from your serverless functions.

* Set Up Unified AWS Access and use AWS (Amazon Web Services) IAM authentication where possible.

  You can connect to your Atlas clusters using AWS (Amazon Web Services) [IAM roles](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/id_roles_use.html)
  instead of hardcoding your credentials in Lambda. Hardcoded credentials are
  viewable by anyone who accesses your AWS Lambda environment, which can pose
  a security risk. With AWS (Amazon Web Services) IAM authentication, Atlas accesses AWS (Amazon Web Services) Lambda
  through an [assumed IAM role](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/using-service-linked-roles.html),
  so you don't need credentials in your connection strings.

  Atlas supports AWS (Amazon Web Services) IAM authentication for clusters running
  MongoDB version 7.0 or higher. We strongly advise using AWS (Amazon Web Services) IAM
  authentication for Lambda connections if your cluster meets the
  requirements.

* The amount of memory allocated to a Lambda function defaults to 128
  MB. You can configure the amount of memory allocated to a Lambda
  function, between 128 MB and 10,240 MB. Ensure you allocate enough
  memory. Increase the memory to increase the amount of virtual CPU
  available and improve MongoDB driver performance. To learn more, see
  [Memory and computing power](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/lambda/latest/operatorguide/computing-power.html).

* Set your [AWS\_STS\_REGIONAL\_ENDPOINTS](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/sdkref/latest/guide/feature-sts-regionalized-endpoints.html) and
  [AWS\_REGION](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/sdkref/latest/guide/feature-region.html) environment
  variables.

codeBlocks.content:
mkdir bref-mongodb-app && cd bref-mongodb-app
composer init
composer require bref/bref bref/extra-php-extensions mongodb/mongodb
vendor/bin/bref init

plugins:
    - ./vendor/bref/bref
    - ./vendor/bref/extra-php-extensions

functions:
    api:
        handler: index.php
        runtime: php-85-fpm
        layers:
            - ${bref-extra:mongodb2-php-85}
